intro: Calculate the final amount to charge clients in seconds. Enter your subtotal, tax rate, and optional discount percentage to get a complete invoice breakdown including tax, discount, and total due.
howItWorks: Discount Amount = Subtotal × Discount%. Taxable Amount = Subtotal − Discount. Tax Amount = Taxable Amount × Tax Rate%. Invoice Total = Taxable Amount + Tax. This follows the standard order: discount is applied before tax, so tax is calculated on the discounted amount. This reduces the tax base and is the correct approach in most jurisdictions.
interpretationGuide: Tax is applied after the discount to keep calculations HMRC/IRS compliant — taxing the discounted amount rather than the full subtotal. If you need to add line items with different tax rates, calculate each line separately and sum the results. Use this calculator for estimates; your accounting software should handle final invoicing.

faqs.question: Should discount be applied before or after tax?
faqs.answer: Discount should be applied before tax in most cases. Tax is then calculated on the reduced taxable amount. This is both the standard practice and compliant with most tax authorities including HMRC and the IRS.

faqs.question: What is VAT in the UK?
faqs.answer: VAT (Value Added Tax) is 20% for standard-rated goods and services, 5% for reduced-rate items (e.g., home energy, children's car seats), and 0% for zero-rated items (most food, children's clothing). If you are VAT-registered, you must add VAT to invoices and file VAT returns.

faqs.question: When do I need to register for VAT?
faqs.answer: In the UK, you must register for VAT when your taxable turnover exceeds £90,000 in a rolling 12-month period (2024 threshold). You can also register voluntarily below this threshold, which lets you reclaim VAT on business expenses.

faqs.question: What information must a UK invoice include?
faqs.answer: A valid UK VAT invoice must include: your business name and address, VAT registration number, invoice number, invoice date, customer name and address, description of goods/services, net amount, VAT rate and amount, and total amount due.

faqs.question: How do I add multiple line items?
faqs.answer: Calculate each line item separately if they have different tax rates. For items with the same tax rate, sum all line items into a subtotal, apply the discount, then apply the tax. Most accounting software (Xero, QuickBooks, FreeAgent) handles this automatically.

faqs.question: What is a proforma invoice?
faqs.answer: A proforma is a preliminary invoice sent before goods or services are delivered. It is not a legally binding document and cannot be used to reclaim VAT. Use a final invoice once the work is complete.

faqs.question: What is the standard payment term for invoices?
faqs.answer: The most common payment term is 30 days (Net 30) from invoice date. Some businesses use 14 days or 60 days. Always state payment terms clearly on the invoice. You are legally entitled to charge interest on late payments in the UK under the Late Payment of Commercial Debts Act.

faqs.question: How do I handle retainers on an invoice?
faqs.answer: Retainers are typically invoiced monthly as a fixed fee. Include a brief description of the services covered, the period (e.g., 'Monthly retainer — October 2024'), the net amount, VAT, and total. Keep a record of work delivered against the retainer.

faqs.question: What is a credit note?
faqs.answer: A credit note is the opposite of an invoice — it reduces the amount owed. Issue a credit note when you need to cancel or partially refund an invoice. It should reference the original invoice number and include the same VAT details.

faqs.question: How do I invoice in foreign currencies?
faqs.answer: You can invoice in any currency. For UK VAT purposes, convert the amount to GBP using the Bank of England exchange rate or the rate on the invoice date. Show both the foreign currency amount and the GBP equivalent on the invoice.

faqs.question: Can I charge interest on late invoices?
faqs.answer: Yes. In the UK, you can charge statutory interest of 8% plus the Bank of England base rate on late B2B invoices under the Late Payment of Commercial Debts Act. Always send a polite reminder first — most late payments are accidental.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a quote and an invoice?
faqs.answer: A quote is an estimate of cost provided before work begins — it is not legally binding unless accepted by the client. An invoice is issued after services are rendered or goods delivered and is a legally binding request for payment.
